technical_risks.risk: Supplier portal integrations may be brittle, restricted by terms, inconsistent across suppliers, or unsafe if credentials are mishandled.
technical_risks.mitigation: Validate initial supplier import strategy in Stage 2, start with file/manual import where possible, and require secure credential architecture before portal automation.

technical_risks.risk: Unified product, service, bundle, recipe, cost, pricing, and quote data models can become too complex if every vertical is supported at launch.
technical_risks.mitigation: Validate one launch vertical and model extensibility without implementing every industry-specific variation in MVP.

technical_risks.risk: Supplier product and service data may have inconsistent fields, missing assets, incomplete costs, service-specific pricing tables, or incompatible category structures.
technical_risks.mitigation: Use field mapping, import review, validation errors, supported templates, and required-field quality checks.

technical_risks.risk: Quote, catalog, proposal, and price book generation can expand into a document design product if output scope is not constrained.
technical_risks.mitigation: Pick one first output type and a small template set for MVP.

technical_risks.risk: AI features depend on clean data, reliable retrieval, cost controls, and clear user trust boundaries.
technical_risks.mitigation: Defer advanced AI until core data quality and workflow usage are validated; start with assistive features only after source data is structured.

business_risks.risk: The platform spans many markets and may dilute MVP value if no initial vertical is selected.
business_risks.mitigation: Stage 2 must validate the first target segment and its highest-friction workflow.

business_risks.risk: Users may expect live inventory, automated ordering, procurement, marketplace, or customer storefront behavior if platform messaging overpromises.
business_risks.mitigation: Position MVP around internal supplier aggregation, cost modeling, pricing, bundling, and sales output generation.

compliance_risks.risk: Supplier account credentials, proprietary pricing, customer-specific pricing, quotes, and margin data are sensitive business information.
compliance_risks.mitigation: Require secure credential storage decisions, access controls, audit needs, retention policy, and encryption requirements in later architecture stages.

compliance_risks.risk: Automated scraping or portal access may conflict with supplier terms.
compliance_risks.mitigation: Review supplier terms before building automated connectors and prefer API, file export, or user-approved import methods.

compliance_risks.risk: AI-generated descriptions, pricing suggestions, or quote outputs may introduce inaccurate claims or pricing errors.
compliance_risks.mitigation: Keep humans in approval loops and label AI output as assistive until validated.

operational_risks.risk: Import support burden may increase if users upload messy supplier files or service pricing tables.
operational_risks.mitigation: Define supported templates, clear validation errors, import review states, and initial vertical-specific onboarding.

operational_risks.risk: Approval workflows for pricing, quotes, and catalog publishing can slow adoption if overbuilt.
operational_risks.mitigation: Start with simple draft, review, approved, and sent states.

constraints.constraint: MVP should not depend on broad automatic supplier portal synchronization.
constraints.reason: Supplier integration surfaces are unknown and likely high variance.

constraints.constraint: MVP must represent both products and services in the asset model.
constraints.reason: The expanded product value depends on combining vendor products and supplier services into resale offerings.

constraints.constraint: MVP should support complete enough cost breakdowns to protect margins.
constraints.reason: Pricing and margin management are core to the product promise.

constraints.constraint: MVP should choose one first customer-facing output type.
constraints.reason: Catalogs, price books, quotes, proposals, online catalogs, and portals each imply different UX and infrastructure.
